title: Defining Nodes and Edges
concept: Mapping graph nodes to LLM calls or tool execution, and edges to transition logic.

exercises.prompt: What do we call the connections between states in a directed graph representing agents?
exercises.explain: Edges connect different nodes in the graph, showing how one state can transition to another.

cards.title: What are Nodes?
cards.body: In a graph, nodes represent specific points or entities. For AI, they can map to calls made to a language model or actions performed by a tool.

cards.title: What are Edges?
cards.body: Edges are the connections between nodes. They define the logic of transitions, showing how you can move from one node to another.

cards.title: Graph as a City Map
cards.body: Think of a graph like a city map. Nodes are the locations (like cafes or libraries), and edges are the roads that connect those places.

cards.title: Example of a Node and Edge
cards.body: A node could be a request to a text generation model, and the edge might be the rule that decides when to send that request.

cards.title: Myth: Nodes and Edges Are Always Physical
cards.body: Some might think nodes and edges are only for physical connections, but they can represent abstract ideas and processes in AI!

cards.title: Visualizing Nodes and Edges
cards.body: In a graph-based system, visualize nodes as points and edges as lines connecting them.

exercises.prompt: What do edges represent in our graph-based orchestration?
exercises.explain: Edges show how to move from one node to another, defining the transition logic of the agent.

exercises.prompt: Nodes are used to represent transitions between different actions in a graph.
exercises.explain: Nodes represent specific actions or calls, not the transitions themselves.

exercises.prompt: In a graph, nodes can represent __________ of a model or tool execution.
exercises.sentence: In a graph, nodes can represent specific actions of a model or tool execution.
exercises.explain: The term 'specific actions' is used to accurately describe what nodes represent in the graph.

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements is an example of a node in a graph?

options.text: A function call to generate text
The logic to repeat an action

exercises.explain: A function call to generate text is a specific action, making it a perfect example of a node.

exercises.prompt: In our orchestration graph, how do nodes and edges work together?

options.text: Nodes can exist without edges, as edges are only for connection.
Edges define the transition logic while nodes represent actions or calls.
Both nodes and edges are used to represent the same thing in a graph.
Edges can be physical paths, while nodes are only virtual actions.

exercises.explain: Edges enable navigation between nodes, connecting the actions represented by the nodes to create a complete orchestration flow.
